title: From The Temporary To The Eternal Lessons From The Life Of The Apostle Paul
scriptureForWeek: Most of the teaching from this week will come from 1 Timothy 6 with a few other cross references. It’s important to note how revolutionary these ideas were at the time around money, eternity, and discipleship. In our world today, even with all its philanthropy, it’s still a pretty revolutionary concept to go to war with mammon and steward your money with an eye on eternity.
weekTheme: Boys spend; Men steward God will provide for us You can’t talk about faith without talking about money. Sometimes this makes people a little nervous. Prosperity preachers have distorted what the Bible teaches about money, taking advantage of people only to make themselves rich. There always seems to be a scandal about some celebrity preacher living the high life in a private jet or expensive car. In the opposite direction, people can go so far that they actually make a vow of poverty. They literally give away their possessions and life by faith, desperately aware of how money can block our vision of God. Some of the great saints you may have heard about such as Saint Francis of Assisi and Mother Teresa were people who lived like this. In between those two perspectives are a ton of ordinary men just trying to figure out how to pay their bills, save some money so they don’t get stuck in debt, and buy the stuff they need. Proverbs 30:7-9 has a great perspective on how to think about money. It says: Give me neither poverty nor riches! Give me just enough to satisfy my needs. For if I grow rich, I may deny you and say, “Who is the LORD?” And if I am too poor, I may steal and thus insult God’s holy name.

days.title: Contentment
days.scripture: 1 TIMOTHY 6:6-8 Yet true godliness with contentment is itself great wealth. After all, we brought nothing with us when we came into the world, and we can’t take anything with us when we leave it. So if we have enough food and clothing, let us be content.
days.reflection: How much money is enough? How much money should a man seek to get in his life? The answer to this question is one of the most important things a man can settle in his heart, but it’s one that has a lot of different answers depending on what you view the purpose of life to be. Tech billionaires would probably tell us, “There is no such thing as too much money.” People trying to get rich would probably tell us, “More, more, more.” People struggling to pay their bills would say, “A bit more.” In almost every scenario, people think that we always need more money. Our culture gets us to think this way too. It makes products that wear out so we have to continually buy them, and it has new trends and new products that we feel like we need to have. No one wants an old cell phone, no one wants an old video game system, and no one wants old clothes that aren’t cool. So how much is enough? Believe it or not, the Bible tells us the actual answer. It says that as long as our basic needs are covered, we should be content. For Paul, that was food and clothing. In the modern world, it’s a bit more complex, but it has the same idea. If we can pay our bills, cover our living expenses, and have enough to do some nice things occasionally and be generous, we should be content with that. But contentment is hard. Our modern world doesn’t have a clue about how to be content. People are trained by society to want a bigger house, a nicer car, cooler vacations, and better toys. It’s a treadmill of more, more, more. Yet Paul says that Christians have a source of happiness and satisfaction that doesn’t come from stuff. Did you catch it at the start of this verse? Yet true godliness with contentment is itself great wealth. We have a source of love, joy, and peace in our lives that never runs out or runs dry. It isn’t dependent on how much we get or how much we make. It’s true wealth. But this is a secret a man has to learn from God, not something the world will teach him. This is what Paul said to the church in Philippi in Philippians 4:12-13: I know how to live on almost nothing or with everything. I have learned the secret of living in every situation, whether it is with a full stomach or empty, with plenty or little. For I can do everything through Christ, who gives me strength. Being able to do all things through Christ isn’t about playing a sports game well; it’s about being content with what God gives us. It’s about power to break free from being a mindless consumer of the stuff that gets in the way of your walk with God. A man must learn to be content. He must learn to trust God for his needs and daily bread and learn to enjoy the true riches of his faith. A godly man is richer than the world will ever know.
questions: If money were no object and you could buy one thing you really wanted, what would it be and why?
In what way do you see our world trying to make us unhappy and discontent with what we have? Are there any places you see this in your own life?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they first learned about being content. How do they avoid both greed and passivity when it comes to money?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Don’t love money; be satisfied with what you have. For God has said, “I will never fail you. I will never abandon you.” So we can say with confidence, “The LORD is my helper, so I will have no fear. What can mere people do to me?” Hebrews 13:5-6 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of being content and not just a consumer. Thank Him for what he has already given you.

days.title: Mammon
days.scripture: 1 TIMOTHY 6:9-10 But people who long to be rich fall into temptation and are trapped by many foolish and harmful desires that plunge them into ruin and destruction. For the love of money is the root of all kinds of evil. And some people, craving money, have wandered from the true faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows.
days.reflection: Jesus talked a lot about money. A whole lot. According to Howard Dayton, there are 500 Bible verses pertaining to the topics of faith and prayer and yet 2,350 Bible verses on money. Jesus said some really strong things about money. He said that where your treasure is, your heart is. Your heart follows your money. Where we put our money directs our attention and affection. In Matthew 6:24, Jesus gave one of his most memorable one-liners when He said, “No one can serve two masters. For you will hate one and love the other; you will be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and be enslaved to money.” Mammon is “abundance without dependence.” It’s an unhealthy desire to get more money and possession than we could ever need to trick us into thinking that we don’t have to depend on God anymore. The Apostle John warned how deadly this is because it pushes the love of God out of our lives. “Do not love this world nor the things it offers you, for when you love the world, you do not have the love of the Father in you. For the world offers only a craving for physical pleasure, a craving for everything we see, and pride in our achievements and possessions. These are not from the Father, but are from this world." (1 John 2:15-16) Why does Jesus give such harsh warnings against money? Paul lists a few reasons: A desire for money tempts and traps us People who long to be rich fall into temptation and are trapped by many foolish and harmful desires that plunge them into ruin and destruction. We become a slave to whatever masters us. If we make money our master, we will be tempted to work more hours, cut corners, cheat, exploit others, and become ruthless to stay ahead. This is incompatible with being a godly man. A desire for money causes us to wander from the faith And some people, craving money, have wandered from the true faith. An unhealthy desire for money makes us lose focus. It makes us seek first the wrong things in our lives. We get our values all out of whack and start neglecting the things that really matter. Not all at once, but slowly. It doesn’t say a man sprints away from the faith, but that he wanders. It happens bit by bit until we have lost our way. A desire for money damages our souls --And some people, craving money, have wandered from the true faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows--desires that plunge them into ruin and destruction. Stuff never satisfies us; only God can. Some of the most rich and famous people in the world are the most miserable. Idols ruin us. They distort us into men we don’t want to be. Living for money more than God will wreck your life. You may think this could never happen to you at your age, but you need to be aware of the danger of mammon. You need to ask God to help you shift your thinking from the temporary to the eternal. Boys think about the here and now; men play the long game. They guard their hearts so they don’t give their lives to things that can be lost in the drama of life. They invest in the eternal. You don’t want to be a slave to mammon; you want to be a steward of true wealth. Resolve in your heart to think like a godly man and you will be a long way there already.
questions: Have you ever felt something block out your desire for God? Has there ever been something you really wanted that made you forget about almost everything else? Would you be willing to share what it is?
Do you think money is evil in itself, or just loving money is evil? Why do these things seem to get confused so often?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they felt the power of mammon affect their heart. What did they want, and how did it make them lose their focus and priorities?

thoughts.verse: Read through this verses slowly. Don’t make your living by extortion or put your hope in stealing. And if your wealth increases, don’t make it the center of your life.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to open your eyes to the temptation of money and to give you a vision of true wealth instead.

days.title: Generosity
days.scripture: 2 CORINTHIANS 9:7-11 You must each decide in your heart how much to give. And don’t give reluctantly or in response to pressure. “For God loves a person who gives cheerfully.” And God will generously provide all you need. Then you will always have everything you need and plenty left over to share with others. As the Scriptures say, “They share freely and give generously to the poor. Their good deeds will be remembered forever.” For God is the one who provides seed for the farmer and then bread to eat. In the same way, He will provide and increase your resources and then produce a great harvest of generosity in you. Yes, you will be enriched in every way so that you can always be generous. And when we take your gifts to those who need them, they will thank God.
days.reflection: What is the first thing that comes to mind when you hear the word generous? Is it someone who looks frustrated? Is it someone super serious? Is it someone who is gritting their teeth and begrudgingly handing something over? Generosity may look very different from what a consumer culture thinks. The Bible says that God loves a cheerful giver. Look at what Paul says in today’s reading. You must each decide in your heart how much to give. And don’t give reluctantly or in response to pressure. “For God loves a person who gives cheerfully.” The Greek word for cheerful is an interesting word. It’s the word ἱλαρός (hilaros). Now, what word does that sound like to you? That’s right, the word hilarious. The Bible says that God loves someone who thinks it hilarious to give. It’s beyond happy, beyond joyful; they think that being able to give is one of the most fun and enjoyable things a man gets to do. In this verse, Paul contrasts two types of givers. Those who give because they are forced to, the “have to” givers, and those who love to give, the “get to" givers. The “have to" givers “Have to" givers have closed hearts. They live with a self-focused mentality that worries about their needs, their desires, and their concerns. They have immature and childish thinking about giving. They ask questions like: How much do I have to give? How much do I have to give so I don’t feel guilty? Will I have to give again, or can I just get away with doing this once? Who is going to be mad at me if I don’t give? I only give because I feel bad. The "get to" givers “Get to” givers have open hearts. They live with a God-focused mentality that worries about the needs, desires, and concerns of others. They have mature thinking about giving. They ask questions like: How much do I get to give? Will I have the joy of being able to give again? Who is going to be blessed by my generosity? Who can I give more generously and intentionally to? I experience deep joy when I get to be generous with others. Generosity isn’t just about sharing our stuff. It’s about our hearts. We are generous with others because of how generous God has been with us. Our generosity reflects God’s generosity. Paul reminded the Corinthians in 2 Corinthians 8:9 about the reasons and root of their generosity: You know the generous grace of our Lord Jesus Christ. Though he was rich, yet for your sakes He became poor, so that by His poverty He could make you rich. We love because He first loved us. We give because He first gave to us. We are generous to others because of how generous God has been to us. As you seek to understand how money works, you are going to realize that being generous is at the heart of manhood. By nature, men want to give to those they love. They want to give their time, their passion, their energy, and their resources to bless other people. Why not ask God to give you a generous heart today.
questions: Who is one of the most generous people you know? Why do you think this? How do they show their generosity?
Have you ever been generous to someone else? What did you do and how did it make you feel?

Ask the person you are doing this study with how they learned to become generous with their lives. What kind of things have they been generous with over the years? (EG: time, money, attention, other resources, etc.)

thoughts.verse: And don’t forget to do good and to share with those in need. These are the sacrifices that please God. Hebrews 13:16 Read through thi verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to remove any selfishness from your heart and to replace it with generosity.

days.title: Sowing And Reaping
days.scripture: 2 CORINTHIANS 9:6-11 Remember this: a farmer who plants only a few seeds will get a small crop. But the one who plants generously will get a generous crop. You must each decide in your heart how much to give. And don’t give reluctantly or in response to pressure. “For God loves a person who gives cheerfully.” And God will generously provide all you need. Then you will always have everything you need and plenty left over to share with others. As the Scriptures say, “They share freely and give generously to the poor. Their good deeds will be remembered forever.” For God is the one who provides seed for the farmer and then bread to eat. In the same way, he will provide and increase your resources and then produce a great harvest of generosity in you. Yes, you will be enriched in every way so that you can always be generous. And when we take your gifts to those who need them, they will thank God.
days.reflection: One of the challenges of living in the modern world is the fact that we have lost touch with the process of how things are made. All we see is the final product, so we can forget that everything comes into being through a process. We are trained to think about the end result, not what happens along the way. But this is deceptive and can distort our understanding of how life works. People who lived in other generations and those that live on farms know that things take time. There is a very important law that many men forget today; one you have to learn to live well as a man. The law of sowing and reaping. Look at what Paul said from today’s reading: Remember this—a farmer who plants only a few seeds will get a small crop. But the one who plants generously will get a generous crop. If you want any sort of harvest, you will have to sow some seed. If you want a bigger harvest, you will have to sow more seed. If you want a different harvest, you will have to sow a different seed. Paul wanted to remind the Corinthians that they didn’t have to give, but they got to give. He didn’t want to force them to become generous people or give what they didn’t have. But he did want them to understand the beauty of generosity and the consequences of selfishness by showing them what happens when you sow a little versus sowing a lot. You need to learn the law of sowing and reaping as you grow into manhood. Don’t be naive and expect something from nothing. Don’t be stingy and sow a small amount of seed. Be wise, sow generously, and you will reap generously. If you talk to any man who is a good steward of their money, they will tell you the best thing about having money is the ability to bless other people and help others by being generous. Proverbs 19:17 says, “Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD, and He will reward them for what they have done.” We don’t need to live with a poverty mentality of lack that thinks we have to keep all we have. If you give your life to Jesus, He promises to supply our needs. Not just our daily bread, but an abundance so we can be generous to others. Look at the promise Paul reminded the Corinthians: And God will generously provide all you need. Then you will always have everything you need and plenty left over to share with others...Yes, you will be enriched in every way so that you can always be generous. And when we take your gifts to those who need them, they will thank God. Resolve in your heart to fight mammon, learn contentment, sow generously, and enjoy the rewards God gives to generous men. That’s the inheritance you really want.
questions: What is the longest you have ever had to wait for something you really wanted? What happened in you during the waiting period, and how did it affect your perception of what you received when you got it?
On a scale of 1-10, how generous would you say you are? What is one small thing you can do this week to cultivate generosity in your heart?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share what they have learned about sowing and reaping. What is an area they sowed a small number of seed and got a small return? What is an area they sowed generously and got a large return?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. You have seen them before in the Primal Path, but look at them in light of what Paul said about sowing and reaping and generosity. Do not be deceived: God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows. Whoever sows to please their flesh, from the flesh will reap destruction; whoever sows to please the Spirit, from the Spirit will reap eternal life. Let us not become weary in doing good, for at the proper time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up. Therefore, as we have opportunity, let us do good to all people, especially to those who belong to the family of believers. Galatians 6:7-10 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a generous heart. Ask him to mature any childish and selfish thinking you may be clinging too. Ask him for a fresh vision of becoming a generous man like Jesus was.

days.title: Rewards
days.scripture: 1 TIMOTHY 6:17-19 Teach those who are rich in this world not to be proud and not to trust in their money, which is so unreliable. Their trust should be in God, who richly gives us all we need for our enjoyment. Tell them to use their money to do good. They should be rich in good works and generous to those in need, always being ready to share with others. By doing this they will be storing up their treasure as a good foundation for the future so that they may experience true life.
days.reflection: One of the biggest misconceptions people have about being a Christian is that you spend your whole life missing out on all the good stuff. The world thinks that our faith is defined by all the things we don’t get to do. It’s true that there are some sinful things that seem fun on the front end, but they don’t lead to long-term joy. In fact, much of what the world values leads to long-term regret. Jesus’ heart is not that you miss out on the good stuff; it’s that you DON’T miss out on the good stuff. So much of Jesus’ teachings are actually about rewards, treasure, and blessing. But the world has distorted the way we see these things, so we don’t understand what really matters. The rewards that the world offers are short-term, fleeting, and immediate. The things Jesus offers are long-term, eternal, and worth waiting for. In today’s passage, Paul is trying to help the Ephesians get this point. Ephesus was a pretty wealthy city, and the church had a lot of rich people in it. Paul was trying to help those wealthy believers to know how to steward their money in a godly way. So, Paul warns them against putting their trust in their money, which is so unreliable, and to learn to put their trust in God. Then they are to “use their money to do good. They should be rich in good works and generous to those in need, always being ready to share with others.” God calls them to be willing to share and be generous with what they had. But something absolutely amazing happens here. Instead of losing their riches and missing out, they actually gain rewards for giving what they have to others. Paul promises three rewards for being generous: First, we lay a solid foundation “...a good foundation for the future" The older you get, the more you are going to realize how up-and-down and unreliable earthly riches can be. The stock market can go up and down, companies and banks can fail, real estate can be destroyed; it’s all pretty fragile. But God’s kingdom is forever, and if you invest in it, your investment will never get destroyed. The Apostle Peter promised in 1 Peter 1:3-4, “Now we live with great expectation, and we have a priceless inheritance—an inheritance that is kept in heaven for you, pure and undefiled, beyond the reach of change and decay.” Beyond the reach and decay of the world and kept by God. That’s a solid investment. Strive to focus on that. Second, we store up treasure in heaven “By doing this they will be storing up their treasure...” Randy Alcorn points out that no one can take their earthly riches with them. You have never seen someone take their mansions and cars with them to the graveyard. But according to the Bible, we can send our treasure ahead. We can invest now in God’s kingdom in such a way that it is waiting for us when we get there. That means for people who don’t know God, the closer they are to death, the further they are from their treasure. But for people who do know God, the closer they get to death, the closer they are to their treasure. This is revolutionary and something godly men understand. Third, we enjoy the life that is truly life NOW “...so that they may experience true life.” True life isn’t found in things. It’s found in relationships and experiences with people we love. Many men end up being financially wealthy but relationally poor. Their stuff has gotten in the way of their faith, friends, and family. But generosity clears out the clutter of mammon so that we can enjoy what truly matters. We serve a generous God. Paul reminds the Ephesians that He “richly gives us all we need for our enjoyment.” Generosity leads to rewards. Start investing in your eternal inheritance now.
questions: Why do you think that Jesus, Paul, and the other leaders in the Bible talk about rewards so much? How often have you thought about your faith and walk on the Primal Path being rewarded by God?
If you could ask God for one reward right now, what would it be? Why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some of the rewards they have experienced through being generous. Ask them how they learned to think about generosity at your age.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Don’t store up treasures here on earth, where moths eat them and rust destroys them, and where thieves break in and steal. Store your treasures in heaven, where moths and rust cannot destroy, and thieves do not break in and steal. Wherever your treasure is, there the desires of your heart will also be. Matthew 6:19-21 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of eternal rewards. Ask Him to help you think long-term satisfaction instead of short-term pleasure.

extras: WEEK 4 MANSCHOOL LEFT HAND/RIGHT HAND MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: LEFT HAND/RIGHT HAND Jesus said it’s better to give than to receive. Jesus said that we should give in secret. This week you are going to find out that what He said is true. Open Matthew 6:1-4 and read it with your son. Then get as much money as you can spare; this could be 20 bucks, 100 bucks, etc. Ask your son if there is anyone in his life that he thinks could use some encouragement or generosity. List out a few names and then think of something you could do to bless them. This could be a gift card and a note, their favorite food, or maybe the cash itself with a message of encouragement. Work on being sneaky in delivering the gift and do as much as you can to ensure you are not found out. This could be dropping it at someone’s house at night or mailing it to them. It could be having a third party deliver it. Get creative. Then, without telling your son, send him a note of encouragement and a small gift telling him you notice his growth and development. OPTION 2. WATCH: PAY IT FORWARD Great story about how a small amount of generosity can change the world. It has a few PG-13 spots in it but has a potent message. You can check out the trailer here: https://youtu.be/qfW0wCV9iFI
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
